sourceText: Authored the hadith collection regarded by Sunni Muslims as the most authentic book after the Quran.
Pioneering Hadith Criticism
Developed and implemented a rigorous methodology ('ilm al-rijal) for authenticating traditions by scrutinizing the biographies and reliability of narrators.
Extensive Scholarship
Authored numerous other works on hadith and Islamic jurisprudence, such as 'Al-Adab al-Mufrad'.
What is a hadith?
A hadith is a report of the sayings, actions, or silent approvals of the Islamic prophet Muhammad. They are a primary source of Islamic law and moral guidance, second only to the Quran.
Why is 'Sahih al-Bukhari' so important?

It is considered the most reliable collection of hadith because of Imam al-Bukhari's incredibly strict criteria for inclusion. He required an unbroken chain of verifiably trustworthy narrators who had met each other, a standard higher than other compilers.
Why was he exiled?

He was exiled due to a theological dispute and professional jealousy. A rival scholar in Nishapur questioned his stance on whether the Quran's recitation was created, a sensitive topic. This was used by the ruler of Bukhara to demand private tutoring, which al-Bukhari refused, leading to his final exile.
Born in the Fergana Valley, Transoxiana (modern-day Uzbekistan).
Moved to Baghdad and began working at the House of Wisdom under the patronage of Caliph al-Ma'mun.
Wrote his most famous work, 'Elements of Astronomy' (Jawāmiʿ ʿIlm al-Nujūm).
Participated in the project to measure the diameter of the Earth in the Syrian desert.
Traveled to Egypt to design and supervise the construction of the Great Nilometer on Rawda Island, Fustat (Old Cairo).
Post 861
His exact death year is unknown, but his last recorded activity was in 861.
Elements of Astronomy
A concise, non-mathematical summary of Ptolemy's Almagest that became a standard textbook in Europe for over 400 years under the name 'Alfraganus'.
Calculation of Earth's Diameter
Participated in a project commissioned by Caliph al-Ma'mun to measure the length of a degree of latitude, yielding a remarkably accurate figure for the Earth's circumference.
The Great Nilometer
Designed and supervised the construction of a sophisticated structure in Cairo to measure the annual flood levels of the Nile River, which remained in use for centuries.
What was Al-Farghani's Latin name?
He was widely known in the Latin West as 'Alfraganus'.
Was Al-Farghani only an astronomer?
No, he was also a skilled engineer, most famously demonstrated by his design and construction of the Nilometer in Cairo.
How influential was his book?
It was a primary university textbook in Europe until the 15th century and influenced major figures like Dante, Sacrobosco, and Regiomontanus.
Where was the House of Wisdom?
The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) was a major intellectual center during the Islamic Golden Age, located in Baghdad.
블라디미르 대공의 아들로 태어남.
블라디미르 대공 사망 후, 형제간의 왕위 계승 전쟁 시작.
알타 강 전투에서 형 스뱌토폴크를 물리치고 키예프 대공이 됨.
최초의 루스 법전인 '루스카야 프라브다'의 초기 버전을 제정함.
키예프 공성전에서 페체네그족을 결정적으로 격파하고 루스의 유일한 통치자가 됨.
승리를 기념하여 키예프에 성 소피아 대성당 건설을 시작함.
다섯 아들에게 영토를 나누어준 후 비슈호로드에서 사망함.
루스카야 프라브다
최초의 동슬라브 법률 체계를 성문화하여 정의와 질서의 기틀을 마련했습니다.
키예프의 황금기
그의 통치는 교육, 건축, 문해력을 증진시키며 키예프 루스의 문화적, 정치적 절정을 이루었습니다.
페체네그 격파
1036년 유목민 페체네그족에게 최종적이고 압도적인 패배를 안겨 루스의 남쪽 국경을 확보했습니다.
유럽의 장인
딸들과 자매들을 프랑스, 노르웨이, 헝가리, 폴란드의 왕가와 전략적으로 결혼시켰습니다.
왜 '현명공'이라고 불리나요?
학교와 도서관을 설립하는 등 문화와 학문을 후원하고, 루스 최초의 성문법인 '루스카야 프라브다'를 제정한 공로 때문입니다.
루스카야 프라브다란 무엇인가요?
키예프 루스의 첫 번째 법전입니다. 개인적인 복수를 벌금 제도로 대체하고 재산, 범죄, 사회적 지위에 대한 법적 원칙을 확립했습니다.
그의 사후 키예프 루스는 어떻게 되었나요?
아들들에게 평화롭게 지내라는 그의 경고에도 불구하고, 그의 죽음은 후손들 사이의 분권화와 내분으로 이어져 결국 국가를 약화시켰습니다.
Born, likely in a village on the Northern Dvina River. His early life is largely undocumented.
Gained notoriety as a Cossack ataman, possibly engaging in piracy and mercenary activities along the Volga and Don rivers.
Hired by the Stroganov merchant family, Yermak leads a force of over 800 Cossacks across the Ural Mountains to confront the Khanate of Sibir.
October 26, 1582
Achieves a decisive victory against Khan Kuchum's forces at the Battle of Chuvash Cape.
Late 1582
Captures Qashliq (Isker), the capital of the Khanate of Sibir, forcing Khan Kuchum to flee into the steppes.
Yermak sends his lieutenant Ivan Koltso to Moscow with news of the conquest and a tribute of furs. Tsar Ivan the Terrible officially pardons the Cossacks and sends reinforcements.
August 6, 1585
Ambushed by Khan Kuchum's forces while camping on an island in the Irtysh River. Yermak drowns, allegedly weighed down by the chainmail armor gifted to him by the Tsar.
Conquest of the Khanate of Sibir
His campaign led to the collapse of the Tatar Khanate, opening up the vast territory for Russian colonization.
Initiation of Russian Expansion into Siberia
Yermak's expedition was the pivotal first step in Russia's eastward expansion, which would eventually stretch to the Pacific Ocean.
National Hero of Russia
Despite his ambiguous origins, he became a legendary figure in Russian folklore and history, celebrated as a national hero who expanded the Russian state.
Was Yermak a pirate?

His early life is poorly documented, but many historical sources and legends suggest he was a river pirate on the Volga before being hired by the Stroganovs. His official pardon from the Tsar for past crimes supports this theory.
Why did Yermak invade Siberia?

He was initially hired by the Stroganov merchant family to protect their settlements from raids by the Siberian Tatars led by Khan Kuchum. This defensive mission quickly turned into an offensive campaign of conquest.
How did Yermak's small force defeat the larger Tatar army?

Yermak's Cossacks had a significant technological advantage with their firearms (muskets). The Tatars, who primarily used bows, arrows, and spears, were often unable to counter the disciplined volleys of the Cossacks.
How did Yermak die?

He was killed during a surprise night attack by Tatar forces in August 1585. While trying to escape to his boat, he drowned in the Irtysh River, supposedly because he was weighed down by the heavy suit of armor the Tsar had given him as a gift.
Born in Antioch to a noble family.
Begins an ascetic life as a hermit in the mountains near Antioch after his mother's death.
Ordained a presbyter (priest) in Antioch and begins his celebrated preaching career.
Consecrated Archbishop of Constantinople against his will, becoming a major figure in the imperial capital.
Condemned and briefly exiled by the Synod of the Oak, orchestrated by his political and ecclesiastical enemies.
Exiled a second time by Empress Eudoxia, this time to Cucusus in Armenia.
Dies during a forced march to a more remote place of exile in Pityus on the Black Sea.
Golden-Mouthed Orator
His eloquent sermons (homilies) on the Bible and Christian morality earned him the posthumous title 'Chrysostom'.
Doctor of the Church
Recognized in both Eastern and Western Christianity for his significant contributions to theology and doctrine.
Champion of the Poor
He was a fierce critic of wealth and luxury, redirecting church funds to build hospitals and aid the poor.
Liturgical Reformer
The Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom is still the primary liturgy used by the Eastern Orthodox Church and Byzantine Rite Catholic Churches.
Why was he called 'Chrysostom'?
The name 'Chrysostomos' is Greek for 'golden-mouthed.' It was a title given to him after his death in recognition of his extraordinary eloquence and power as a preacher.
What was his conflict with Empress Eudoxia about?

He publicly denounced the extravagance and perceived immorality of the imperial court. Empress Eudoxia felt his sermons were direct attacks on her, leading to her intense animosity and his eventual exile.
What is his most famous work?

Among his many writings, his 'Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom' is still the primary eucharistic service of the Eastern Orthodox Church. His numerous homilies (sermons) on books of the Bible are also widely studied.
Why did he become a hermit?
Influenced by the early Christian monastic movement, he sought a life of spiritual discipline, prayer, and contemplation, removed from the distractions and temptations of city life.
Born Mīrzā Muhammad Tāraghay bin Shāhrukh in Soltaniyeh, Persia.
Appointed as the governor of Samarkand at the age of 16 by his father, Shah Rukh.
Begins construction of the Ulugh Beg Madrasa (an Islamic school) on the Registan square in Samarkand.
Builds the great Ulugh Beg Observatory in Samarkand to advance the study of astronomy.
Completes the 'Zij-i Sultani' (Sultanic Star Tables), a monumental star catalogue and astronomical treatise.
Ascends to the throne of the Timurid Empire after his father Shah Rukh's death, leading to a period of instability.
Defeated in battle by his rebellious son, Abd al-Latif Mirza, and subsequently assassinated near Samarkand.
Ulugh Beg Observatory
Constructed one of the finest observatories in the Islamic world, featuring a massive Fakhri sextant with a radius of about 36 meters.
Zij-i Sultani Star Catalogue
A highly accurate star catalogue of 1,018 stars, considered the most precise of its time and for nearly 200 years after.
Mathematical Advances
Made significant contributions to trigonometry and spherical geometry, calculating sine and tangent tables to eight decimal places of accuracy.
Was Ulugh Beg a good ruler?

His rule as the Timurid Sultan was short and troubled. While a great patron of arts and sciences, he was not a skilled military commander or politician, which led to his swift downfall and assassination.
What was so special about his observatory?
It was a true research institute, not just an observation post. Its main instrument, a gigantic masonry sextant, allowed for unprecedented precision in measuring the positions of celestial bodies.
How did his work influence later science?

His star catalogue was translated and circulated, reaching Europe by the 17th century. It provided a crucial dataset for astronomers like Tycho Brahe and was considered the most important star catalogue between Ptolemy and Brahe.
